channel.title: Green Curve Insights
channel.description: Daily ESG and climate compliance intelligence for Indian businesses.

item.title: CPCB Notice to E-Waste Recyclers and Producers: GST-Linked E-Invoicing Now Mandatory for EPR Compliance
item.description: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) issued a critical notice on June 15, 2023, mandating that e-waste recyclers must now use GST-linked e-invoices for all sales and purchases of recovered materials and e-waste in order to generate valid EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility) Certificates on t
item.content:encoded: <h3>Executive Summary</h3><p>The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) issued a critical notice on June 15, 2023, mandating that e-waste recyclers must now use GST-linked e-invoices for all sales and purchases of recovered materials and e-waste in order to generate valid EPR (Extended Producer Responsibility) Certificates on the EPR Portal. Producers are simultaneously required to accept only GST-linked invoices from recyclers as legally recognised proof of recycling. This effectively closes a documentation loophole and tightens the audit trail across India's formal e-waste recycling chain.</p><h3>What Changed</h3><p>The E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022, notified by the Ministry of Environment, Forest &amp; Climate Change on November 2, 2022, established a digital EPR Portal through which recyclers generate EPR Certificates — the instruments that allow producers to demonstrate they have met their mandatory e-waste collection and recycling targets. Until this notice, the documentation standards for transactions on the EPR Portal were not explicitly harmonised with India's GST e-invoicing framework. The CPCB's June 15, 2023 directive now makes GST-linked e-invoices the sole acceptable documentation for: (1) recyclers recording the purchase of e-waste and the sale of recovered/end-product materials on the EPR Portal, and (2) producers accepting proof of recycling from those recyclers. In plain terms: if a recycler cannot show a GST e-invoice for their e-waste transactions, they cannot generate a legitimate EPR Certificate. And if a producer accepts non-GST documentation from a recycler, that recycling activity will not count toward the producer's EPR obligation.</p><h3>Who Is Affected</h3><ul><li>Registered E-Waste Recyclers: All recyclers authorised under the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 who process and recover materials from electrical and electronic equipment (EEE).</li><li>Producers of Electrical and Electronic Equipment (EEE): Any company — domestic or multinational — that manufactures, imports, or sells EEE products listed in Schedule I of the 2022 Rules (covering consumer electronics, IT equipment, lighting, medical devices, etc.).</li><li>Producer Responsibility Organisations (PROs): Entities that facilitate EPR compliance on behalf of multiple producers and coordinate with recyclers — their documentation flows are directly impacted.</li><li>Bulk Consumers and Collection Agents: While the notice is addressed to recyclers and producers, bulk consumers who channel e-waste to recyclers should ensure their own transaction records are GST-compliant to maintain a clean chain of custody.</li><li>Large and Mid-Sized Manufacturers: Companies with significant annual production volumes and correspondingly large EPR targets face the highest financial exposure if recycling claims are invalidated due to non-compliant invoicing.</li><li>SME Recyclers: Smaller recycling units that may not yet be fully integrated into the GST e-invoicing system face the greatest operational adjustment and risk of EPR Certificate invalidation.</li></ul><h3>Key Obligations & Deadlines</h3><ul><li>IMMEDIATE — Recyclers must transition all sales and purchase transactions related to e-waste and recovered/end-product materials to GST e-invoices generated through the GST Portal. Non-GST or manual invoices will no longer be valid for EPR Portal submissions.</li><li>IMMEDIATE — Recyclers must link these GST e-invoices to their records on the CPCB EPR Portal as the basis for generating EPR Certificates. Ensure your EPR Portal account is configured to upload or reference GST invoice data.</li><li>IMMEDIATE — Producers must update their internal procurement and compliance verification processes to accept only GST-linked sales invoices from e-waste recyclers as valid proof of recycling toward EPR targets.</li><li>ONGOING — Producers and PROs must audit existing recycler agreements and contracts to include a contractual requirement for GST e-invoice compliance, creating a legal basis for rejecting non-compliant documentation.</li><li>COMPLIANCE REVIEW — Companies should conduct an internal audit of all EPR Certificates already generated and recycling claims already submitted in FY 2022-23 to assess whether documentation meets the new GST-linked standard, and proactively engage CPCB if gaps are identified.</li><li>REGISTRATION CHECK — Recyclers not yet registered for GST e-invoicing (applicable to businesses with annual turnover above the notified threshold) must complete registration immediately. Those below the threshold should seek legal clarity on applicability.</li><li>DEADLINE CONTEXT — While no grace period is specified in the notice (effective June 15, 2023), EPR targets for FY 2023-24 will be assessed against this standard; non-compliant recycling transactions risk being excluded from target calculations.</li></ul><h3>Climate Transition Angle</h3><p>E-waste is one of the fastest-growing waste streams globally, and the recovery of critical minerals — lithium, cobalt, copper, rare earth elements — from end-of-life electronics is increasingly central to the climate transition. India's ability to build a circular economy for these materials directly supports domestic manufacturing of EVs, solar panels, and energy storage systems, reducing dependence on virgin mineral extraction and the associated carbon footprint. This CPCB notice, while procedural in nature, is part of a broader global tightening of traceability standards in recycling and resource recovery. The European Union's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and the ISSB's IFRS S2 climate disclosure standards both require companies to account for material risks and circularity in their supply chains. For multinational producers selling into India, failure to maintain rigorous, auditable EPR compliance records creates a supply chain transparency gap that will increasingly be scrutinised by global investors and regulators. From a physical risk perspective, India's dependence on imported critical minerals makes domestic recycling infrastructure a strategic climate resilience asset. Strengthening the formal recycling sector through enforceable documentation standards — as this notice does — is a foundational step toward reducing that import dependency and building supply chain resilience against resource scarcity scenarios projected under 2°C and higher warming pathways. Companies with Science-Based Targets (SBTs) or net-zero commitments should also note that scope 3 emissions accounting increasingly includes the end-of-life treatment of sold products. A credible, GST-verified EPR compliance record provides the documentation backbone for such scope 3 disclosures.</p><h3>What To Do Now</h3><ul><li>Recyclers: Register or verify your GST e-invoicing capability immediately — contact your GST Suvidha Provider (GSP) or the GST Portal helpdesk. Ensure your billing software or ERP system is configured to generate e-invoices for all e-waste purchase and recovered material sale transactions, and that invoice reference numbers can be uploaded to the CPCB EPR Portal.</li><li>Producers &amp; PROs: Issue a formal compliance advisory to all your registered recycling partners requiring GST e-invoice submission with immediate effect. Update vendor onboarding checklists and recycler empanelment criteria to make GST e-invoice compliance a mandatory qualification.</li><li>Legal &amp; Compliance Teams: Amend recycler contracts and PRO agreements to include a clause explicitly requiring GST e-invoice documentation for all EPR-related transactions, with a right to reject non-compliant claims. Specify that the producer/PRO bears no liability for EPR shortfalls arising from a recycler's failure to comply.</li><li>Sustainability &amp; Finance Teams: Cross-check your FY 2022-23 EPR Certificate portfolio against the new documentation standard. If any certificates were generated without GST-linked invoices, consult with your legal team and consider voluntary disclosure to CPCB to avoid penalties during the next compliance assessment cycle.</li><li>IT &amp; Data Teams: Build a data linkage between your GST invoice repository and your EPR Portal submissions to enable real-time verification and streamline annual EPR reporting. This integration will also support future ESG and sustainability disclosures that require auditable recycling data.</li></ul><h3>Our Take</h3><p>This notice may read like a technical clarification, but its significance should not be underestimated. By anchoring EPR Certificate generation to the GST e-invoicing infrastructure, CPCB is effectively bringing the weight of India's tax enforcement architecture to bear on e-waste compliance — a smart and overdue move. The informal recycling sector has long been a weak link in India's e-waste chain, with a substantial portion of e-waste being processed outside the formal system. Requiring GST e-invoices creates a hard filter: recyclers operating informally or without proper GST registration simply cannot generate valid EPR Certificates, which in turn means producers cannot use their output to meet EPR targets. For producers, this raises the stakes considerably — your EPR compliance is now only as strong as your recyclers' GST compliance. We expect CPCB to follow this notice with stricter scrutiny of EPR Portal submissions and potentially enhanced penalties for producers found to have claimed recycling credits based on non-GST documentation. Companies that proactively audit their recycler networks and upgrade their documentation systems now will be well-positioned — those that wait risk EPR shortfalls, penalties, and reputational exposure as India's ESG regulatory environment continues to harden.</p>

item.title: Beta Framework Testing: What India's Business Leaders Need to Know About TNFD's Nature Disclosure Roadmap
item.description: The Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures (TNFD) released its beta framework for market testing, inviting companies and financial institutions to pilot nature-related risk assessment and disclosure practices ahead of the finalised framework. For Indian businesses — particularly those in
item.content:encoded: <h3>Executive Summary</h3><p>The Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures (TNFD) released its beta framework for market testing, inviting companies and financial institutions to pilot nature-related risk assessment and disclosure practices ahead of the finalised framework. For Indian businesses — particularly those in agriculture, textiles, mining, pharmaceuticals, and financial services — early engagement with the TNFD beta process is not optional preparation; it is a strategic imperative. As global capital markets increasingly price nature-related risks alongside climate risks, companies that build disclosure capabilities now will hold a decisive advantage when mandatory or investor-driven reporting requirements arrive.</p><h3>What Changed</h3><p>The TNFD (Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures) is a global, market-led initiative that has developed a framework to help organisations identify, assess, manage, and disclose their dependencies and impacts on nature — covering biodiversity, freshwater, land, and ocean systems. Modelled structurally on the Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD), the TNFD framework was released in iterative beta versions (v0.1 through v0.4) between 2022 and 2023, inviting businesses worldwide to stress-test the methodology in real operating environments before the final framework was published in September 2023. The beta testing phase was significant because it moved nature-related financial risk from a theoretical concern into a practical, structured disclosure discipline. Companies that participated in beta testing worked through the LEAP approach — Locate your interface with nature, Evaluate dependencies and impacts, Assess material risks and opportunities, and Prepare to respond and report. This iterative, open-testing model allowed the TNFD to refine sector-specific guidance, data requirements, and metrics based on real-world feedback from over 500 pilot organisations globally.</p><h3>Who Is Affected</h3><ul><li>Agribusiness, food &amp; beverage companies with land-use footprints and supply chain dependencies on ecosystems</li><li>Textile and apparel manufacturers reliant on water-intensive cotton and natural fibres</li><li>Mining and extractives companies with operations in or near biodiversity-sensitive zones</li><li>Pharmaceutical and biotechnology companies dependent on natural compounds and genetic resources</li><li>Infrastructure and real estate developers whose projects intersect with natural habitats</li><li>Banks, NBFCs, and institutional investors financing sectors with significant nature dependencies</li><li>Export-oriented businesses supplying to EU, UK, and US markets where nature disclosure is becoming a procurement and regulatory requirement</li><li>Large listed companies likely to face SEBI BRSR (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting) evolution toward nature metrics</li></ul><h3>Key Obligations & Deadlines</h3><ul><li>Familiarise leadership and sustainability teams with the final TNFD framework (published September 2023) and the sector-specific supplemental guidance available at tnfd.global</li><li>Conduct an initial LEAP assessment: map where your operations and supply chains interface with nature-sensitive areas using tools such as the TNFD's recommended IBAT (Integrated Biodiversity Assessment Tool) and WWF's STAR metric</li><li>Review SEBI's BRSR Core disclosures (mandatory for top 150 listed companies from FY2023-24) — nature-adjacent metrics on water, waste, and biodiversity are already embedded and will likely expand</li><li>Monitor the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS E4 — Biodiversity &amp; Ecosystems), which will affect Indian companies with EU operations or large EU customer bases</li><li>Engage with pilot cohorts and industry associations (such as CII, FICCI sustainability councils) running TNFD familiarisation workshops to benchmark against Indian peers</li><li>Begin building internal data infrastructure for nature-related metrics — species impact, freshwater consumption by watershed, land-use change — as these will underpin future disclosures</li><li>No single statutory deadline in India as of now, but EU CSRD obligations for large non-EU subsidiaries begin phasing in from 2026; proactive alignment is advised immediately</li></ul><h3>Climate Transition Angle</h3><p>Nature and climate are inseparable systems, and the TNFD framework explicitly recognises this interdependence. Physical climate risks — extreme heat, erratic monsoons, flooding, and groundwater depletion — are simultaneously nature risks for Indian businesses. A textile manufacturer in Gujarat facing water scarcity is experiencing both a climate physical risk and a nature dependency risk. The TNFD framework provides the analytical architecture to assess these compounding risks together, rather than in silos. Globally, the TNFD aligns with and complements the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) framework — particularly IFRS S2 on climate — and is being integrated into CSRD's ESRS standards. Indian companies reporting under ISSB-aligned standards (which SEBI is expected to progressively adopt) will find that nature disclosure is the logical next layer after climate disclosure. The Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework, agreed at COP15 in 2022, set a target of 30% protected land and oceans by 2030 and requires financial flows to be aligned with biodiversity goals — creating regulatory and reputational pressure that will flow through to corporate supply chains and investment mandates. Early TNFD adoption positions Indian businesses ahead of this tightening landscape.</p><h3>What To Do Now</h3><ul><li>ASSIGN OWNERSHIP: Designate a cross-functional TNFD working group including sustainability, finance, risk, and procurement leads — nature risk is not solely a CSR function; it requires CFO and board-level visibility</li><li>RUN A MATERIALITY SCREENING: Use the TNFD's LEAP approach to conduct a rapid, high-level screening of your top three to five value chain nodes for nature dependencies and impacts — this scoping exercise can be completed in 6 to 8 weeks with existing data</li><li>AUDIT YOUR DATA GAPS: Identify which nature-related metrics (water withdrawal by source, land-use change, supplier geographic coordinates near protected areas) you currently do not capture, and initiate data collection protocols in your next reporting cycle</li><li>ENGAGE YOUR SUPPLY CHAIN: Nature risks are overwhelmingly concentrated in upstream supply chains — begin supplier engagement and questionnaires on land use, water stewardship, and proximity to Key Biodiversity Areas (KBAs)</li><li>ALIGN WITH BRSR AND PLAN AHEAD: Map TNFD disclosure requirements against your current BRSR Core submissions to identify overlap, efficiency opportunities, and gaps — use this as your roadmap for the next two to three annual reporting cycles</li></ul><h3>Our Take</h3><p>The TNFD beta framework testing phase was a quiet but consequential moment in the evolution of sustainable finance — it signalled that nature-related financial risk is transitioning from voluntary best practice to the foundational layer of corporate disclosure, sitting alongside climate as a non-negotiable lens for investors, lenders, and regulators. For Indian businesses, the urgency is real but the window to act proactively is still open. India's economy is among the most nature-dependent in the world: agriculture employs over 40% of the workforce, water stress affects major industrial corridors, and biodiversity loss threatens supply chains from pharmaceuticals to food. Companies that treat TNFD preparation as a compliance checkbox are missing the strategic point — those that genuinely integrate nature risk into capital allocation, procurement decisions, and long-term strategy will be better positioned for resilience, investor confidence, and access to green finance. The question is no longer whether nature disclosure will become mandatory for significant Indian enterprises; it is how quickly, and whether your organisation will be ready.</p>

item.title: How to Set Science-Based Targets: A Practical Guide for Indian Businesses
item.description: Science-based targets (SBTs) are rapidly becoming the global benchmark for credible corporate climate commitments, with the Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi) providing the most widely recognised framework for setting emissions reduction goals aligned with limiting global warming to 1.5°C. For
item.content:encoded: <h3>Executive Summary</h3><p>Science-based targets (SBTs) are rapidly becoming the global benchmark for credible corporate climate commitments, with the Science Based Targets initiative (SBTi) providing the most widely recognised framework for setting emissions reduction goals aligned with limiting global warming to 1.5°C. For Indian companies — whether responding to investor pressure, supply chain requirements from multinational customers, or upcoming domestic disclosure mandates — understanding how to set and validate science-based targets is no longer optional. This post walks sustainability and compliance teams through the SBTi process, its obligations, and why acting now positions your organisation ahead of the regulatory and market curve.</p><h3>What Changed</h3><p>The Science Based Targets initiative is not a government regulation — it is a global standard-setting body backed by CDP, the United Nations Global Compact, World Resources Institute (WRI), and WWF. However, its Corporate Standard has effectively become the de facto international framework for corporate emissions target-setting, and its requirements are increasingly being referenced in mandatory regulations worldwide, including the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) and the ISSB's IFRS S2 climate disclosure standard. The SBTi requires companies to set greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions reduction targets across Scope 1 (direct emissions), Scope 2 (purchased energy), and, critically, Scope 3 (value chain emissions) that are consistent with the level of decarbonisation required to keep global temperature rise to 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels. Targets are independently validated by SBTi's technical team and, once approved, must be publicly disclosed and progress reported annually. As of 2024, over 7,000 companies globally have committed to or validated targets through SBTi. In India, the number remains relatively small but is growing rapidly, driven by export-oriented sectors, listed companies with foreign institutional investors, and businesses embedded in the supply chains of global corporations that have made their own SBTi commitments.</p><h3>Who Is Affected</h3><ul><li>Large Indian listed companies subject to SEBI's Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR) Core disclosure requirements, particularly those in the top 1,000 listed entities</li><li>Export-oriented manufacturers — especially in textiles, pharmaceuticals, chemicals, auto components, and IT services — whose global customers require supply chain emissions disclosures or SBTi alignment</li><li>Indian subsidiaries or affiliates of multinational corporations that have made SBTi commitments and must account for Scope 3 supply chain emissions</li><li>Companies seeking foreign institutional investment or green financing, where SBTi validation is increasingly used as a credibility signal by ESG-focused investors</li><li>SMEs and mid-cap companies that are tier-1 or tier-2 suppliers to large domestic or global corporations with SBTi commitments</li><li>Financial institutions and banks that are setting their own financed emissions targets and may require portfolio companies to demonstrate credible climate commitments</li></ul><h3>Key Obligations & Deadlines</h3><ul><li>COMMITMENT LETTER: Submit a commitment letter to SBTi via their online portal, publicly pledging to set science-based targets — companies then have 24 months to develop and submit validated targets</li><li>GHG INVENTORY (PREREQUISITE): Complete a comprehensive GHG inventory covering Scope 1, Scope 2, and Scope 3 emissions using the GHG Protocol Corporate Standard — this is required before targets can be set and must use a base year no earlier than 2015</li><li>SCOPE 3 MATERIALITY SCREENING: Identify which of the 15 Scope 3 categories are material (typically those representing more than 40% of total emissions) — SBTi requires Scope 3 targets if Scope 3 emissions exceed 40% of total footprint</li><li>TARGET SETTING: Use SBTi-approved methods — the Absolute Contraction Approach (ACA) requiring at minimum 4.2% absolute reduction per year for Scope 1 and 2, or sector-specific pathways where available (e.g., Steel, Cement, ICT, Financial Institutions)</li><li>SUBMISSION &amp; VALIDATION: Submit targets to SBTi for independent validation — as of 2024, SBTi charges a validation fee (USD 4,950 for companies with revenue above USD 250 million; lower tiers for SMEs) and the review process typically takes 10–14 weeks</li><li>ANNUAL PROGRESS DISCLOSURE: Once validated, companies must report progress against targets annually through public channels (e.g., CDP disclosure, annual reports, sustainability reports) — failure to report can result in targets being delisted</li><li>NET-ZERO STANDARD (OPTIONAL BUT RECOMMENDED): Companies may also set long-term net-zero targets under the SBTi Net-Zero Standard, requiring 90–95% absolute emissions reduction by 2050 across all scopes, with residual emissions addressed through carbon removal (not offsets)</li></ul><h3>Climate Transition Angle</h3><p>Science-based targets sit at the intersection of physical climate risk and transition risk management. On the transition side, as carbon pricing mechanisms, border carbon adjustments (such as the EU's Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism, or CBAM, which came into force in 2023), and mandatory climate disclosure rules proliferate globally, companies without credible decarbonisation roadmaps will face higher cost of capital, loss of market access, and reputational damage. SBTi validation provides a third-party verified signal that a company's climate strategy is grounded in science, not marketing. For Indian companies specifically, the connection to global standards is direct and urgent. The ISSB's IFRS S2 standard — which India's SEBI has signalled it will align with in future iterations of BRSR — requires companies to disclose their transition plans and emissions reduction targets. SBTi provides a ready-made, globally recognised methodology that satisfies the 'target-setting' component of these disclosure frameworks. Similarly, the EU's CSRD requires European companies to assess and disclose the climate ambition of their value chain partners — meaning Indian exporters will face questionnaires and contractual requirements from European customers that effectively mandate SBTi-aligned thinking. On physical risk, the process of setting science-based targets forces organisations to deeply understand their emissions hotspots — which are often also their exposure points to physical climate risk. A company that discovers 60% of its Scope 3 emissions come from agricultural raw material inputs, for example, is simultaneously identifying a major physical climate risk (crop yield disruption) and a decarbonisation priority. SBTi target-setting, done well, is therefore an integrated risk management exercise, not just a compliance checkbox.</p><h3>What To Do Now</h3><ul><li>CONDUCT A GHG INVENTORY IMMEDIATELY: If your organisation does not have a verified Scope 1, 2, and 3 GHG inventory, commission one now using the GHG Protocol standard — this is the non-negotiable foundation for everything that follows and typically takes 3–6 months for a first-time exercise</li><li>ENGAGE YOUR BOARD AND CFO: Science-based targets require capital allocation commitments — renewable energy procurement, supply chain transformation, process efficiency investments. Secure board-level buy-in early and integrate target-setting into your capital expenditure planning cycle</li><li>IDENTIFY YOUR SECTOR PATHWAY: Check whether SBTi has a sector-specific standard for your industry (available for Steel, Cement, Apparel, ICT, Financial Institutions, and others) — sector pathways provide more tailored and often more achievable glide paths than generic absolute contraction methods</li><li>SUBMIT YOUR COMMITMENT LETTER WITHIN THE NEXT QUARTER: Submitting the commitment letter is free, signals climate ambition to investors and customers immediately, and starts the 24-month clock — giving your team structured time to develop robust targets without being rushed</li><li>MAP YOUR SCOPE 3 VALUE CHAIN NOW: Begin engaging key suppliers, logistics partners, and raw material providers for emissions data — Scope 3 data collection is the most time-consuming part of the process, and early supplier engagement builds the relationships needed for credible supply chain decarbonisation</li></ul><h3>Our Take</h3><p>Setting science-based targets is quickly transitioning from a voluntary best practice to a de facto market requirement for any Indian company operating at scale in global markets or seeking international capital. What makes SBTi uniquely powerful — and uniquely demanding — is that it does not allow companies to set whatever target feels comfortable; it anchors ambition to what climate science actually requires. For Indian businesses accustomed to setting internal sustainability KPIs, this can feel like a significant shift in approach, and it is. But that rigour is also its value: an SBTi-validated target carries credibility with investors, regulators, and customers that self-declared targets simply do not. Looking ahead, as SEBI tightens BRSR assurance requirements and India moves toward mandatory climate risk disclosures aligned with ISSB standards, companies that have already embedded SBTi methodology into their strategy will be structurally better positioned — both to comply with less effort and to genuinely lead the transition in their sectors. The window to be an early mover in India is still open, but it is closing faster than most boards appreciate.</p>

item.title: Understanding GRI: The Global Standard-Setter Shaping Corporate Sustainability Disclosure in India
item.description: The Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) is the world's most widely adopted sustainability reporting framework, providing companies with a structured, stakeholder-focused methodology to disclose environmental, social, and governance (ESG) impacts. As India's regulatory landscape evolves — with SEBI's B
item.content:encoded: <h3>Executive Summary</h3><p>The Global Reporting Initiative (GRI) is the world's most widely adopted sustainability reporting framework, providing companies with a structured, stakeholder-focused methodology to disclose environmental, social, and governance (ESG) impacts. As India's regulatory landscape evolves — with SEBI's Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR) increasingly aligning with global norms — understanding GRI's foundations is no longer optional for Indian businesses seeking credibility with international investors and supply chain partners. Companies that proactively adopt GRI Standards position themselves ahead of tightening disclosure mandates and build lasting trust with stakeholders.</p><h3>What Changed</h3><p>The Global Reporting Initiative was founded in 1997 in the aftermath of the Exxon Valdez oil spill, with a mandate to create a common language for organisations to report on their sustainability impacts. Over nearly three decades, GRI has evolved from a niche environmental reporting guideline into the de facto global baseline for sustainability disclosure, used by over 10,000 organisations across 100+ countries. In 2021, GRI released its landmark updated Universal Standards — GRI 1 (Foundation), GRI 2 (General Disclosures), and GRI 3 (Material Topics) — which came into mandatory effect for GRI reporters from January 1, 2023. These updated standards shifted the framework's philosophy firmly toward 'impact materiality': requiring companies to report on how their operations affect the economy, environment, and people — not just how ESG factors affect the company financially. This is a critically important distinction that sets GRI apart from financially-oriented frameworks like ISSB/IFRS S1 and S2. GRI operates as an independent international organisation headquartered in Amsterdam, with a global network of offices and partners. It collaborates formally with the ISSB, EFRAG (which governs CSRD in Europe), and the UN, making it a central pillar of the global sustainability disclosure architecture that Indian companies must navigate.</p><h3>Who Is Affected</h3><ul><li>Large listed Indian companies (NSE/BSE) subject to SEBI's BRSR Core requirements, where GRI alignment can streamline compliance</li><li>Indian subsidiaries of multinational corporations headquartered in the EU, UK, or US — where parent companies face mandatory GRI-aligned or GRI-compatible reporting obligations under CSRD, UK SDR, or SEC climate rules</li><li>Export-oriented manufacturers and suppliers whose global customers (especially in Europe and North America) require GRI-based sustainability disclosures as part of supply chain due diligence</li><li>Indian companies seeking international ESG-linked financing, green bonds, or sustainability-linked loans — where lenders and rating agencies use GRI disclosures as a benchmark</li><li>Mid-sized companies preparing voluntarily for future regulatory expansion, particularly as SEBI has indicated BRSR will be extended beyond the top 1,000 listed companies</li><li>ESG consultants, CFOs, sustainability officers, and board-level risk committees across sectors including manufacturing, IT, pharmaceuticals, infrastructure, and financial services</li></ul><h3>Key Obligations & Deadlines</h3><ul><li>GRI Universal Standards 2021 (GRI 1, 2, and 3) are now mandatory for all organisations claiming to report 'in accordance with GRI Standards' — this has been in effect since January 1, 2023</li><li>Companies must conduct a formal materiality assessment aligned with GRI 3 to identify and prioritise material topics before selecting applicable topic-specific standards (e.g., GRI 302 for Energy, GRI 305 for Emissions, GRI 403 for Occupational Health &amp; Safety)</li><li>GRI 2 requires comprehensive general disclosures including governance structure, strategy, policies, and stakeholder engagement — all of which must be updated to reflect the 2021 standards in any report published after January 1, 2023</li><li>Organisations reporting under GRI must publish a GRI content index — a structured table mapping each disclosure to the relevant section of the report — to ensure verifiability and comparability</li><li>While GRI reporting itself is voluntary globally (except where mandated by specific jurisdictions), SEBI's BRSR framework explicitly references GRI Standards as an acceptable international framework for supplementary disclosures, creating a de facto compliance pathway for Indian listed companies</li><li>Companies seeking to align with CSRD (applicable to Indian subsidiaries of EU-headquartered firms or large Indian companies with EU operations) must note that ESRS standards have significant overlap with GRI — early GRI adoption reduces future compliance burden</li><li>No single global statutory deadline for GRI adoption exists, but companies with EU nexus face CSRD reporting timelines beginning FY2025 for the largest companies, cascading to smaller entities by FY2026-2028</li></ul><h3>Climate Transition Angle</h3><p>GRI's relevance to India's climate transition cannot be overstated. The framework's topic-specific standards — particularly GRI 305 (Emissions), GRI 302 (Energy), GRI 303 (Water), and GRI 201 (Economic Performance) — provide the granular disclosure architecture that investors, regulators, and civil society need to assess whether Indian companies are genuinely transitioning to low-carbon business models or merely engaging in greenwashing. Critically, GRI's impact materiality approach is complementary — not competitive — to the ISSB's financial materiality approach (IFRS S1 and S2). The ISSB and GRI signed a formal collaboration agreement in 2022, establishing that the two frameworks together form a 'building blocks' approach to comprehensive sustainability reporting. For Indian companies, this means GRI disclosures on how operations impact climate systems (e.g., Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions) can be paired with ISSB-aligned disclosures on how climate risks impact financial performance — giving stakeholders a complete picture. From a physical risk perspective, GRI disclosures on water stress, biodiversity, and supply chain impacts are increasingly material for Indian companies in sectors like agriculture, textiles, and cement, which are acutely exposed to monsoon variability, heat stress, and resource scarcity. Investors using TCFD-aligned analysis increasingly cross-reference GRI disclosures to validate physical risk assessments. As India advances its National Action Plan on Climate Change and deepens its NDC commitments, GRI-aligned reporting will be a key mechanism for demonstrating private sector accountability in the transition.</p><h3>What To Do Now</h3><ul><li>Conduct a GRI-aligned double materiality assessment: Map your company's significant impacts on the environment and society (impact materiality) alongside the ESG risks and opportunities that affect your financial performance (financial materiality). This single exercise anchors both GRI and ISSB/BRSR Core compliance simultaneously.</li><li>Audit your existing BRSR disclosures against GRI 2 (General Disclosures) and GRI 3 (Material Topics): Many Indian companies will find significant overlap — identify gaps systematically and build a disclosure roadmap that satisfies both SEBI and GRI requirements without duplicating effort.</li><li>Identify applicable GRI topic-specific standards for your sector: Use GRI's Sector Standards (currently available for Oil &amp; Gas, Coal, Agriculture, Mining, and Financial Services, with more in development) to ensure your disclosures reflect industry-specific material topics that generic templates miss.</li><li>Engage your supply chain and value chain partners on GRI-aligned data collection: Scope 3 emissions, supplier labour practices, and water usage across the value chain are increasingly required under GRI 308, GRI 414, and GRI 305-3. Begin building data systems and supplier questionnaires now, before regulatory pressure makes this urgent.</li><li>Seek third-party assurance on key GRI disclosures: As SEBI has mandated reasonable assurance for BRSR Core metrics, aligning your GRI content index with assured data points builds investor credibility and pre-empts future mandatory assurance requirements under evolving Indian and global regulations.</li></ul><h3>Our Take</h3><p>GRI is not just a reporting framework — it is the foundational language of global sustainability accountability, and Indian companies that treat it as a peripheral, voluntary exercise are underestimating a significant strategic risk. We are entering a period where sustainability disclosures are rapidly transitioning from voluntary best practice to regulatory requirement, and the companies that have invested in robust GRI-aligned systems will adapt to each new mandate — BRSR Core, CSRD, ISSB — with speed and confidence, while their competitors scramble. What makes GRI particularly powerful for Indian businesses is its dual utility: it satisfies domestic regulators who reference it in BRSR guidance, and it satisfies international investors and supply chain customers who have been using it for decades. The next five years will see India's disclosure ecosystem converge significantly with global standards. Our view is that GRI adoption today is not compliance overhead — it is competitive infrastructure.</p>

item.title: EFRAG & EU CSRD: What Indian Companies Exporting to Europe Must Know Now
item.description: The European Union's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), underpinned by standards developed by EFRAG (European Financial Reporting Advisory Group), is rapidly becoming the most consequential sustainability disclosure regime in the world — and its reach extends well beyond European b
item.content:encoded: <h3>Executive Summary</h3><p>The European Union's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), underpinned by standards developed by EFRAG (European Financial Reporting Advisory Group), is rapidly becoming the most consequential sustainability disclosure regime in the world — and its reach extends well beyond European borders. Indian companies supplying goods or services to EU-based corporations, or those listed on EU-regulated markets, are already within scope of its value chain disclosure requirements. Companies that treat CSRD as a purely European problem do so at significant commercial risk.</p><h3>What Changed</h3><p>The CSRD replaced the earlier Non-Financial Reporting Directive (NFRD) and dramatically expanded both the scope of companies required to report and the depth of information they must disclose. The European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS), developed by EFRAG and adopted by the European Commission, form the technical backbone of CSRD reporting. These standards cover a sweeping range of topics — climate change (physical and transition risks), biodiversity, water, pollution, workforce conditions, supply chain due diligence, and governance. Crucially, ESRS E1 (Climate) requires large EU companies to disclose their full value chain emissions — Scope 1, 2, and 3 — along with transition plans, scenario analyses, and climate-related financial risks. This means EU buyers will increasingly demand verified emissions data, sustainability performance metrics, and evidence of credible decarbonisation commitments from their Indian suppliers. EFRAG continues to refine sector-specific standards and guidance for Small and Medium Enterprises (SMEs), signalling that the regulatory framework is still evolving and tightening.</p><h3>Who Is Affected</h3><ul><li>Large Indian exporters supplying directly to EU-listed or EU-headquartered companies in sectors such as textiles, pharmaceuticals, automotive components, IT services, chemicals, and agri-commodities</li><li>Indian subsidiaries or joint ventures of European parent companies, which may be required to provide consolidated sustainability data</li><li>Indian companies with securities listed on EU-regulated markets or those raising capital from European institutional investors</li><li>Mid-sized Indian manufacturers embedded in EU company supply chains — even if not directly reporting under CSRD, they will face contractual data requests from EU customers</li><li>Indian IT and professional services firms providing ESG data management, assurance, or consulting services to European clients</li><li>Indian banks and financial institutions with significant exposure to EU-linked corporates or green bond markets</li></ul><h3>Key Obligations & Deadlines</h3><ul><li>January 2025 (FY2024 data): Large EU public-interest entities (&gt;500 employees, already under NFRD) must publish first CSRD-compliant reports — their Scope 3 data requests to Indian suppliers begin immediately</li><li>January 2026 (FY2025 data): All large EU companies (meeting two of three thresholds: &gt;250 employees, &gt;€40M turnover, &gt;€20M total assets) must report under CSRD — significantly expanding the pool of EU buyers requesting value chain ESG data from Indian partners</li><li>January 2027 (FY2026 data): Listed SMEs on EU-regulated markets come into scope — relevant for Indian companies cross-listed in Europe</li><li>Ongoing — EFRAG Sector Standards: Sector-specific ESRS (covering oil &amp; gas, mining, textiles, food &amp; agriculture, and others) are under development; Indian companies in these sectors should monitor EFRAG's publication calendar closely</li><li>Ongoing — Double Materiality Assessments: EU companies must conduct 'double materiality' assessments identifying both how sustainability issues affect the business AND how the business impacts society and environment — Indian suppliers will be asked to contribute data to these assessments</li><li>Immediate — Contractual ESG clauses: EU buyers are already embedding CSRD-aligned data-sharing requirements into supplier contracts and RFP processes; Indian companies should review new and renewal contracts carefully</li></ul><h3>Climate Transition Angle</h3><p>CSRD and the EFRAG standards are not merely a reporting exercise — they are a structural mechanism to align European corporate behaviour with the EU's 2050 net-zero target and the Paris Agreement's 1.5°C pathway. For Indian companies, this creates a dual imperative. First, physical climate risk: ESRS E1 requires EU companies to assess and disclose climate-related physical risks across their supply chains. Indian manufacturing hubs — particularly in coastal regions, river basins, and heat-stressed geographies — face material physical risks from extreme weather, water stress, and sea-level rise. EU buyers will scrutinise whether their Indian suppliers have assessed and are managing these risks, as supply chain disruptions become a financial disclosure item for European companies. Second, transition risk and global standard convergence: CSRD's architecture closely mirrors the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) framework — specifically IFRS S2 on climate — and has significant overlap with the Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD). India's own SEBI Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR) Core framework, and RBI's emerging climate risk guidance, are also converging toward these global standards. Indian companies that build CSRD-compatible reporting capabilities will simultaneously be better positioned for domestic BRSR compliance and ISSB-aligned disclosures as India's regulatory expectations rise.</p><h3>What To Do Now</h3><ul><li>Map your EU exposure immediately: Identify all EU-domiciled customers, investors, and business partners. Determine which of them fall under CSRD's first and second waves of reporting — these entities will begin requesting supplier ESG data in 2025 and 2026 respectively</li><li>Establish a Scope 3 emissions baseline: Begin measuring your Greenhouse Gas (GHG) emissions across all three scopes using the GHG Protocol. EU buyers will require credible, ideally third-party verified emissions data. Engage a reputable assurance provider familiar with both GHG Protocol and ESRS requirements</li><li>Conduct a gap assessment against ESRS E1 and ESRS S1/S2: Review your current sustainability disclosures against the European Sustainability Reporting Standards — particularly on climate (E1), your own workforce (S1), and workers in your value chain (S2). Identify material gaps in data collection, governance, and reporting infrastructure</li><li>Engage legal and procurement teams on contract review: Ensure your legal and procurement teams are reviewing new EU customer contracts for sustainability data-sharing clauses, audit rights, and representations. Negotiate reasonable timelines and data specifications before signing</li><li>Align internal reporting systems with ISSB/ESRS architecture: Invest in ESG data management systems that can produce structured, auditable sustainability data aligned with both ESRS and ISSB IFRS S1/S2. This dual-standard compatibility will future-proof your reporting for both EU market access and domestic SEBI BRSR Core requirements</li></ul><h3>Our Take</h3><p>CSRD is not on the horizon for Indian businesses — it is already at the door. The first wave of EU companies began reporting on FY2024 data in early 2025, meaning their Scope 3 supply chain data requests are live today. What makes CSRD particularly significant compared to previous sustainability initiatives is its legal enforceability: non-compliant EU companies face penalties, and they will pass that compliance pressure directly down their supply chains through contractual requirements, procurement scoring, and supplier audits. For Indian companies, the window to treat sustainability as a voluntary communications exercise has closed. Those who move now to build credible, data-driven sustainability reporting capabilities will gain a durable competitive advantage in EU markets — and will find themselves well-prepared for the tightening domestic regulatory environment that SEBI, RBI, and MCA are clearly signalling. Those who wait will face contract losses, market access barriers, and a far steeper — and more expensive — remediation curve.</p>

item.title: ISSB and IFRS Sustainability Standards: What Indian Companies Must Know Before the Global Disclosure Wave Hits
item.description: The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB), operating under the IFRS Foundation, has established two landmark global standards — IFRS S1 and IFRS S2 — that are rapidly becoming the baseline for sustainability and climate-related financial disclosures worldwide. Indian companies with int
item.content:encoded: <h3>Executive Summary</h3><p>The International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB), operating under the IFRS Foundation, has established two landmark global standards — IFRS S1 and IFRS S2 — that are rapidly becoming the baseline for sustainability and climate-related financial disclosures worldwide. Indian companies with international operations, foreign investors, or global supply chain relationships are already feeling indirect pressure to align with these standards, even before a domestic mandate arrives. With SEBI's Business Responsibility and Sustainability Report (BRSR) framework increasingly converging toward ISSB principles, and major trading partners like the EU and UK adopting compatible regimes, the window for proactive preparation is narrowing fast.</p><h3>What Changed</h3><p>The ISSB was established in 2021 at COP26 in Glasgow and released its first two standards — IFRS S1 and IFRS S2 — in June 2023, marking a historic consolidation of fragmented sustainability reporting frameworks (TCFD, SASB, CDSB, and others) into a single, coherent global baseline. IFRS S1 sets out general requirements for companies to disclose material information about sustainability-related risks and opportunities across their entire value chain. It requires companies to report on governance, strategy, risk management, and metrics and targets — the same four-pillar architecture that underpins TCFD. IFRS S2 goes further, specifically addressing climate-related disclosures, including both physical risks (floods, droughts, extreme heat) and transition risks (carbon pricing, stranded assets, shifting market demand). Crucially, IFRS S2 mandates Scope 1, 2, and 3 greenhouse gas emissions disclosure and scenario analysis — tools that many Indian companies are only beginning to adopt. The International Accounting Standards Board (IASB) works in parallel to ensure that sustainability disclosures under ISSB standards are connected to and consistent with financial statements prepared under IFRS Accounting Standards, creating an integrated view of a company's financial health and climate exposure. This 'connectivity' principle is a significant shift from treating sustainability as a separate, non-financial exercise — it places climate risk squarely inside the financial reporting infrastructure.</p><h3>Who Is Affected</h3><ul><li>NSE/BSE-listed companies in the top 1,000 by market capitalisation already subject to SEBI's BRSR Core disclosures — ISSB alignment is the logical next step</li><li>Indian subsidiaries and joint ventures of multinational corporations headquartered in jurisdictions adopting ISSB-aligned standards (EU, UK, Australia, Japan, Singapore, Canada)</li><li>Export-oriented manufacturers supplying to European or North American customers subject to CSRD or SEC climate disclosure rules — Scope 3 supplier data will be demanded</li><li>Indian companies seeking international capital — foreign institutional investors (FIIs) and ESG-focused funds increasingly screen on ISSB-aligned metrics</li><li>Financial institutions, banks, and asset managers whose loan books and portfolios carry climate-exposed assets — physical and transition risk assessment is increasingly expected</li><li>Mid-size companies in high-emitting sectors: steel, cement, chemicals, textiles, automotive, and real estate, regardless of current listing status, as supply chain disclosure obligations cascade downward</li></ul><h3>Key Obligations & Deadlines</h3><ul><li>IFRS S1 &amp; S2 effective date: Annual reporting periods beginning on or after 1 January 2024 — jurisdictions adopting these standards are now in their first reporting cycle</li><li>India (SEBI BRSR Core): Top 150 listed companies must provide reasonable assurance on BRSR Core KPIs from FY 2023-24; top 1,000 companies follow on a comply-or-explain basis — ISSB concepts are embedded in these KPIs</li><li>Scope 1 &amp; 2 GHG emissions disclosure: Required under IFRS S2 from Year 1 of adoption; Scope 3 disclosures permitted one-year relief in most jurisdictions but expected from Year 2</li><li>Climate scenario analysis: Companies must disclose how their business strategy performs under at least two climate scenarios, including a 1.5°C or 2°C pathway — many Indian companies have not yet conducted this exercise</li><li>Industry-specific metrics: IFRS S2 references SASB industry standards for sector-specific disclosures; companies should identify their applicable SASB standard immediately</li><li>Value chain disclosures: Companies must use 'reasonable and supportable' information available about their full value chain — not just owned operations — placing new data demands on procurement and supplier management teams</li><li>Third-party assurance: While not universally mandated on Day 1, jurisdictions and investors are moving rapidly toward requiring limited and then reasonable assurance on sustainability disclosures — build internal controls now</li></ul><h3>Climate Transition Angle</h3><p>ISSB standards are not a reporting exercise in isolation — they are the financial architecture of the global climate transition. By requiring companies to disclose how climate risks affect their cash flows, asset values, and cost of capital, IFRS S1 and S2 create the conditions under which capital markets can price climate risk accurately and redirect investment toward resilient, low-carbon business models. For Indian companies, the physical risk dimension is particularly urgent. India faces some of the world's most severe climate hazards — extreme heat affecting labour productivity, erratic monsoons disrupting agricultural supply chains, coastal flooding threatening port and logistics infrastructure, and water scarcity constraining manufacturing. IFRS S2's requirement to assess and disclose physical risks means companies can no longer treat these as external, unquantifiable events — they must be integrated into financial planning and asset valuation. On the transition risk side, India's own Net Zero commitments, the carbon border adjustment mechanisms being introduced by the EU (CBAM), and the global shift in investor preferences are creating a new competitive landscape. Companies that can demonstrate credible transition plans — with science-based targets, capital expenditure aligned to decarbonisation, and transparent Scope 3 emissions data — will access cheaper capital and retain international customers. Those that cannot will face higher financing costs, supply chain exclusion, and reputational risk. ISSB standards are the language in which this competitive differentiation will be expressed. The convergence between ISSB, CSRD (EU), SEC climate rules (USA), and SEBI BRSR is not accidental — it reflects a coordinated global effort to make climate risk visible in financial markets. Indian companies that prepare for ISSB alignment are simultaneously positioning themselves for compliance across multiple regulatory regimes.</p><h3>What To Do Now</h3><ul><li>Conduct a gap assessment: Map your current BRSR, GRI, or TCFD disclosures against the four pillars of IFRS S1 and S2 (Governance, Strategy, Risk Management, Metrics &amp; Targets) to identify disclosure gaps and data deficiencies — prioritise Scope 3 and scenario analysis as the highest-gap areas for most Indian companies</li><li>Establish your GHG emissions baseline: If you have not yet completed a comprehensive GHG inventory covering Scope 1, 2, and material Scope 3 categories, commission one immediately using the GHG Protocol Corporate Standard — this is the non-negotiable foundation for IFRS S2 compliance and investor credibility</li><li>Run at least one climate scenario: Engage a sustainability advisory firm or use publicly available tools (IEA, NGFS scenarios) to assess how your business strategy holds up under a 1.5°C transition scenario and a high-physical-risk scenario — document assumptions, data sources, and financial implications</li><li>Engage your CFO and Board: ISSB standards explicitly require Board-level governance of climate risk — sustainability cannot remain siloed in CSR or EHS teams. Brief your Audit Committee on IFRS S2 requirements, assign a Board member with climate oversight responsibility, and integrate climate risk into your Enterprise Risk Management (ERM) framework</li><li>Map your supply chain exposure: Identify Tier 1 and Tier 2 suppliers in high-emission or climate-vulnerable sectors, begin collecting activity data, and develop a supplier engagement programme — both because IFRS S2 requires it and because your multinational customers will demand it within 12–24 months</li></ul><h3>Our Take</h3><p>The ISSB standards represent the most significant development in corporate reporting since the introduction of mandatory financial auditing — and their arrival in India's disclosure ecosystem is a matter of 'when,' not 'if.' SEBI has made clear through successive iterations of BRSR that India's large listed companies will be held to globally comparable sustainability disclosure standards, and the ISSB framework is the international benchmark against which Indian regulation will continue to converge. More immediately, the pressure is coming not from domestic regulators but from global capital markets: foreign institutional investors managing trillions of dollars are embedding ISSB alignment into their due diligence checklists, and Indian companies that cannot provide credible, assured, ISSB-compatible data risk being screened out of ESG mandates and green bond markets. Our view is that the next 18 to 24 months are the critical preparation window — companies that invest now in GHG accounting infrastructure, scenario analysis capabilities, and Board-level climate governance will not only achieve compliance but will build a genuine competitive advantage as climate disclosure becomes as routine and expected as financial reporting.</p>

item.title: The GHG Protocol Corporate Standard: India's Essential Framework for Credible Carbon Accounting
item.description: The GHG Protocol Corporate Accounting and Reporting Standard is the globally accepted foundation for measuring and disclosing a company's greenhouse gas emissions — and it underpins virtually every major climate disclosure framework adopted or referenced in India today, including SEBI's Business Res
item.content:encoded: <h3>Executive Summary</h3><p>The GHG Protocol Corporate Accounting and Reporting Standard is the globally accepted foundation for measuring and disclosing a company's greenhouse gas emissions — and it underpins virtually every major climate disclosure framework adopted or referenced in India today, including SEBI's Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting (BRSR), the ISSB's IFRS S2, and the EU's CSRD. Indian companies that have not yet structured their emissions inventory around GHG Protocol principles are carrying significant regulatory, reputational, and investor-relations risk as mandatory disclosure requirements tighten. Understanding and implementing this standard is no longer optional — it is the price of participation in global capital markets and supply chains.</p><h3>What Changed</h3><p>The GHG Protocol Corporate Standard, originally published in 2001 and updated in 2004 by the World Resources Institute (WRI) and the World Business Council for Sustainable Development (WBCSD), establishes the rules of the road for how companies identify, calculate, and report their greenhouse gas emissions. It defines the three-scope framework that has become universal: Scope 1 (direct emissions from owned or controlled sources), Scope 2 (indirect emissions from purchased energy), and Scope 3 (all other indirect emissions across the value chain). While the standard itself has not been recently revised, its global relevance has never been greater. The ISSB's IFRS S2 Climate-related Disclosures standard — which India's Institute of Chartered Accountants (ICAI) and SEBI are actively monitoring for adoption — explicitly requires GHG Protocol-aligned accounting. SEBI's BRSR Core framework, mandatory for the top 150 listed companies from FY2023-24 and expanding further, requires Scope 1 and Scope 2 disclosures and encourages Scope 3 reporting. In short, the Corporate Standard is the technical bedrock beneath all of these obligations, and any gaps in how a company applies it will cascade into non-compliance across multiple frameworks simultaneously.</p><h3>Who Is Affected</h3><ul><li>Top 1,000 listed companies in India subject to SEBI's BRSR reporting requirements, particularly the top 150 for whom BRSR Core assurance is already mandatory</li><li>Large unlisted companies preparing for voluntary ESG disclosures or anticipating future mandatory requirements under Companies Act amendments</li><li>Exporters and manufacturers supplying to EU customers subject to CSRD supply chain due diligence, including textiles, chemicals, steel, cement, and auto-component sectors</li><li>Indian subsidiaries of multinational corporations whose parent companies report under CDP, TCFD, or ISSB-aligned frameworks</li><li>Financial institutions — banks, insurers, and asset managers — required to disclose financed emissions (Scope 3, Category 15) under emerging RBI and SEBI guidance</li><li>Infrastructure and energy companies seeking green finance, sustainability-linked loans, or green bond certification, where third-party GHG verification is a prerequisite</li></ul><h3>Key Obligations & Deadlines</h3><ul><li>BRSR Core (FY2023-24 onwards): Top 150 NSE/BSE-listed companies must disclose assured Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions — these must be calculated using GHG Protocol-consistent methodologies to withstand third-party assurance scrutiny</li><li>BRSR Core (FY2024-25 onwards): Mandatory scope expands to top 250 listed companies; Scope 3 disclosures are encouraged and expected to become mandatory in subsequent phases — begin Scope 3 screening now</li><li>CDP Reporting Cycles (annual, typically April–July submission window): CDP questionnaires for Climate, Water, and Forests are explicitly built on GHG Protocol standards; companies with institutional investor or customer requests must align fully</li><li>ISSB IFRS S2 (anticipated Indian adoption horizon 2025-2027): Requires disclosure of gross Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions with GHG Protocol as the default methodology; companies should treat current BRSR compliance as a stepping stone, not a ceiling</li><li>EU CSRD Supply Chain Obligations (phased from 2024-2026): Indian suppliers to EU-based companies will face contractual data requests for Scope 1, 2, and relevant Scope 3 categories — non-compliant suppliers risk losing contracts</li><li>Establish or audit your organisational boundary: Choose consolidation approach (equity share, financial control, or operational control) and document the rationale — this decision affects the entire emissions inventory</li><li>Set a base year: Select a representative historical year for emissions benchmarking, following GHG Protocol's base year recalculation policy, to enable credible year-on-year progress tracking</li></ul><h3>Climate Transition Angle</h3><p>The GHG Protocol Corporate Standard is not merely a compliance tool — it is the measurement infrastructure of the entire climate transition economy. Without accurate, comparable, and assured emissions data structured around its three-scope framework, it is impossible to set credible Science-Based Targets (SBTs), participate meaningfully in carbon markets, or demonstrate alignment with a 1.5°C pathway to investors and customers. For Indian companies, the transition angle is particularly acute on two dimensions. First, physical risk: as climate impacts intensify — extreme heat, monsoon disruption, water stress — investors and lenders are demanding emissions data as a proxy for transition readiness. A company that cannot account for its own carbon footprint signals broader strategic vulnerability. Second, supply chain pressure: India's export-oriented sectors face mounting due diligence requirements from global buyers in the EU, UK, and US, all of whom are under their own disclosure mandates. The GHG Protocol is the common language that makes cross-border supply chain emissions reporting legible. Global frameworks including ISSB IFRS S2, the EU's ESRS E1 under CSRD, and the SEC's climate disclosure rules (for US-listed entities) all converge on GHG Protocol as the accounting standard. Indian companies that build robust GHG Protocol-aligned systems now are not just meeting today's compliance bar — they are building the data infrastructure for net-zero target-setting, internal carbon pricing, and climate-integrated capital allocation decisions that will define competitive advantage over the next decade.</p><h3>What To Do Now</h3><ul><li>Conduct a GHG Protocol Readiness Assessment: Audit your current emissions inventory against the Corporate Standard's requirements — check organisational boundary definition, scope categorisation, emission factor sources, and data quality. Identify gaps before your next BRSR or CDP submission cycle.</li><li>Build a Scope 3 Screening Inventory: Even if Scope 3 is not yet mandatory for your company, conduct a high-level screening of all 15 Scope 3 categories using the GHG Protocol Scope 3 Standard. Identify your top three to five material categories — for most Indian manufacturers, this will include purchased goods and services (Category 1), use of sold products (Category 11), and business travel or employee commuting.</li><li>Engage Your CFO and Internal Audit Function: Emissions data quality and boundary-setting decisions have financial materiality implications. Bring financial control teams into the GHG accounting governance structure now, ahead of mandatory assurance requirements expanding under BRSR Core.</li><li>Select and Document Emission Factors Transparently: Use credible, jurisdiction-appropriate emission factors — the Central Electricity Authority (CEA) grid emission factor for Indian electricity, IPCC factors for fuels, and supplier-specific data where available. Document sources and update annually. Inconsistent emission factor use is the most common reason for assurance qualifications.</li><li>Develop a Multi-Year Disclosure Roadmap: Map your current GHG accounting maturity against the requirements of BRSR Core, ISSB IFRS S2, and any customer-driven CSRD obligations. Set internal milestones for achieving Scope 3 readiness, third-party verification, and ultimately Science-Based Target submission within a 24-36 month horizon.</li></ul><h3>Our Take</h3><p>The GHG Protocol Corporate Standard occupies a unique position in the ESG landscape: it is simultaneously the most foundational and most under-implemented standard in Indian corporate sustainability practice. Dozens of companies publish BRSR reports with Scope 1 and Scope 2 numbers, yet when examined closely, the organisational boundaries are poorly defined, emission factors are outdated or misapplied, and Scope 3 is either absent or superficially disclosed. This is not a minor technical deficiency — it is a strategic liability. As SEBI expands BRSR Core assurance requirements, as institutional investors deploy GHG data in portfolio-level climate risk modelling, and as EU customers activate supply chain due diligence provisions, the cost of inadequate GHG accounting will shift from reputational to financial with remarkable speed. Our strong view is that Indian companies should treat GHG Protocol implementation not as a compliance checkbox but as the foundation of a broader climate intelligence system — one that informs capital allocation, supplier engagement, product strategy, and investor communication. The companies that invest in this infrastructure today will not just be compliant; they will be positioned to lead.</p>

item.title: India's INDC at 10: What the 2015 Climate Pledge Means for Indian Business in 2025 and Beyond
item.description: In October 2015, India formally submitted its Intended Nationally Determined Contributions (INDC) to the UNFCCC ahead of the Paris COP21, committing to three landmark targets by 2030: reducing the emissions intensity of GDP by 33–35% from 2005 levels, achieving 40% of installed electricity capacity
item.content:encoded: <h3>Executive Summary</h3><p>In October 2015, India formally submitted its Intended Nationally Determined Contributions (INDC) to the UNFCCC ahead of the Paris COP21, committing to three landmark targets by 2030: reducing the emissions intensity of GDP by 33–35% from 2005 levels, achieving 40% of installed electricity capacity from non-fossil fuel sources, and creating an additional carbon sink of 2.5–3 billion tonnes of CO₂ equivalent through forest and tree cover. These commitments — rooted in India's philosophy of 'Climate Justice' — have since become the architectural blueprint for every major climate regulation, energy transition policy, and ESG disclosure requirement that Indian businesses face today. Understanding the INDC is not a matter of historical interest; it is essential context for every corporate sustainability strategy operating in India through 2030 and beyond.</p><h3>What Changed</h3><p>The INDC framework emerged from a decade-long evolution in international climate diplomacy. The 1997 Kyoto Protocol placed binding emission reduction targets only on developed (Annex I) nations. Developing countries, including India, were not legally obligated to cut emissions. This began to shift at the 2007 Bali COP with the introduction of Nationally Appropriate Mitigation Actions (NAMAs) for developing nations, followed by voluntary pledges at Copenhagen (2009) and Cancun (2010). India's pre-2015 pledge — a 20–25% reduction in the emissions intensity of GDP by 2020 compared to 2005 levels — was voluntary and widely recognised as achievable. By 2014, UNEP's Emissions Gap Report confirmed India was on track. The 2013 Warsaw and 2014 Lima COPs introduced the INDC concept: all countries, developed and developing alike, would now prepare and submit nationally determined climate plans ahead of the Paris Agreement. Crucially, the Lima decision clarified that INDCs need not be limited to mitigation — they could encompass adaptation, finance needs, technology transfer, and capacity building. India's INDC, submitted on 2 October 2015 and presented at a press conference by the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC), embraced this broader framing. India's submission explicitly acknowledged the country's development imperatives — poverty eradication, housing for all, universal electricity access — while simultaneously scaling up climate ambition by 75% over its 2020 pledge. This balance between equity and ambition defines India's climate posture to this day. In plain terms: India told the world it would grow, and it would grow cleaner. Per capita emissions in 2030 were projected to remain well below the current average of developed nations (8.98 metric tonnes of CO₂ equivalent). India's share of historical global emissions since 1850 stood at just 3%, compared to 29% for the USA and 45% for other developed countries — a central argument for 'Climate Justice' as the moral framing of the INDC.</p><h3>Who Is Affected</h3><ul><li>Energy &amp; Power Sector: Utilities, independent power producers, and energy-intensive industries are directly affected by the 40% non-fossil fuel installed capacity target — implying a fundamental restructuring of India's energy mix by 2030.</li><li>Heavy Industry &amp; Manufacturing: Steel, cement, chemicals, aluminium, and other high-emission sectors face structural pressure from the GDP emissions intensity target (33–35% reduction by 2030), which rewards energy efficiency and low-carbon production.</li><li>Automotive &amp; Transport: The push for a sustainable green transportation network and solarisation of fuel infrastructure signals long-term demand shifts away from fossil-fuel vehicles.</li><li>Real Estate &amp; Urban Infrastructure: Smart Cities Mission, Housing for All, and climate-resilient urban centre commitments translate into green building codes, energy performance standards, and low-carbon construction mandates.</li><li>Agriculture &amp; Land Use: The carbon sink target (2.5–3 billion tonnes CO₂ equivalent via forests and tree cover) and Green India Mission affect agribusinesses, plantation companies, and land-intensive industries operating near forest land.</li><li>Financial Services &amp; Investors: Banks, insurers, and institutional investors financing Indian infrastructure and industry are exposed to stranded asset risk in fossil fuel-heavy portfolios and transition opportunity in renewables.</li><li>All Listed Companies (Top 1000 by market capitalisation): SEBI's Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting (BRSR) framework — a direct downstream consequence of INDC commitments — requires disclosure of emissions, energy use, and transition plans.</li><li>Export-Oriented Businesses: Companies supplying to EU markets face the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM), which is calibrated against exactly the kind of emissions intensity metrics India committed to reducing in its INDC.</li></ul><h3>Key Obligations & Deadlines</h3><ul><li>INDC Target 1 — Emissions Intensity: Reduce the emissions intensity of GDP by 33–35% by 2030 compared to 2005 levels. (India's Updated NDC, submitted 2022, raised this to 45%.) Baseline year: 2005. Compliance horizon: December 2030.</li><li>INDC Target 2 — Non-Fossil Fuel Capacity: Achieve 40% of cumulative electric power installed capacity from non-fossil fuel-based energy sources by 2030. (Updated NDC raised this to 50%.) Current status: India crossed 40% ahead of schedule in 2023.</li><li>INDC Target 3 — Carbon Sink: Create an additional carbon sink of 2.5–3 billion tonnes of CO₂ equivalent through additional forest and tree cover by 2030. Implementation vehicles: Green India Mission, Green Highways Policy (1% of project cost earmarked for plantation), Namami Gange Mission riverside plantation.</li><li>Renewable Energy Milestone — 175 GW by 2022: Wind (60 GW), Solar (100 GW), Biomass (10 GW), Small Hydel (5 GW). This was subsequently revised upward to 500 GW of non-fossil capacity by 2030 under the Updated NDC.</li><li>BRSR Core Disclosure (SEBI, FY2023-24 onwards): Top 150 listed companies by market cap must provide assured BRSR Core disclosures, including Scope 1 and Scope 2 greenhouse gas emissions, energy consumption intensity, and water use — all directly traceable to INDC metrics.</li><li>Energy Conservation (Amendment) Act, 2022: Introduces carbon trading, energy conservation standards for non-energy sector industries, and obligations for designated consumers — all operationalising the INDC's emissions intensity trajectory.</li><li>Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS), 2023: MoEFCC/BEE framework establishing India's domestic carbon market, with Perform Achieve and Trade (PAT) scheme as the foundation. Obligated entities must meet specific energy consumption norms or purchase carbon credits.</li><li>Forest Conservation (Amendment) Act, 2023: Redefines forest land and affects carbon sink obligations — companies with land banks or supply chains near forest areas must reassess exposure.</li></ul><h3>Climate Transition Angle</h3><p>India's INDC is the foundational document of the country's entire climate transition architecture. Every regulation, standard, and incentive that has followed — from the BRSR framework to the Carbon Credit Trading Scheme to the Production Linked Incentive for solar manufacturing — is an operational instrument designed to deliver on these three 2030 targets. For business leaders, this means the INDC is not a government document to file away; it is the strategic map of where Indian policy, infrastructure investment, and regulatory pressure are headed. From a physical risk perspective, the INDC itself acknowledged India's acute vulnerability: the country has 2.4% of global land area but 17.5% of the world's human population. Climate impacts — heatwaves, erratic monsoons, flooding, and water stress — threaten agricultural output, labour productivity, and infrastructure stability. The adaptation components of the INDC (climate-resilient urban centres, river cleaning, sustainable agriculture) signal that companies with operations, suppliers, or customers in climate-vulnerable geographies must build physical risk assessments into their enterprise risk management frameworks. On global alignment: India's INDC targets map directly onto the metrics required under the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB) IFRS S2 standard (climate-related disclosures) and the EU's Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD), both of which require companies to disclose against nationally and scientifically derived transition pathways. Indian subsidiaries of multinationals and Indian companies with EU market exposure must demonstrate alignment between their Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions trajectories and India's NDC pathway. The EU's Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM), now in its transitional phase, will impose carbon costs on Indian exports of steel, aluminium, cement, fertilisers, and electricity from 2026 — priced precisely against the kind of emissions intensity India committed to reducing. Companies that do not accelerate their own decarbonisation risk paying a carbon tariff that their low-carbon competitors will avoid. The International Solar Alliance, anchored by India as envisioned in the INDC, has since grown into a 120-nation body — signalling that India's climate commitments are not merely domestic but are reshaping global energy trade and technology cooperation in ways that will create new market opportunities for Indian clean-tech and renewable energy companies.</p><h3>What To Do Now</h3><ul><li>Map your emissions baseline to 2005: The INDC uses 2005 as its reference year. Compliance teams and sustainability officers should establish or verify their company's own 2005 emissions intensity baseline (Scope 1 and 2 at minimum), enabling you to demonstrate alignment — or flag divergence — with India's national trajectory. This is also required for BRSR Core disclosures and will be essential for future climate scenario analysis under ISSB IFRS S2.</li><li>Conduct a stranded asset and transition risk audit: Identify all capital expenditure in fossil fuel-dependent assets (coal-based captive power, fossil fuel fleet, high-emission process equipment) with economic life extending beyond 2030. Model these against India's 45% emissions intensity reduction target and a 1.5°C-aligned scenario. Assets that become economically unviable under the transition are stranded assets — and investors and lenders are increasingly pricing this risk.</li><li>Accelerate renewable energy procurement: The 40% (now 50%) non-fossil capacity target has already been met at the national grid level, but corporate power purchase agreements (PPAs), open access solar, and green hydrogen sourcing strategies must be formalised now. Waiting until 2028–2029 will mean competing for limited renewable capacity in an already constrained market. Engage your energy procurement team to develop a five-year renewable transition roadmap.</li><li>Engage your supply chain on forest and land-use risk: The carbon sink target and the Forest Conservation Amendment Act create regulatory and reputational exposure for any company sourcing from or operating near forest land.
